title: Provider Configuration Plane Semantic Contract
description: Source-backed draft semantic contract for the provider configuration plane capability, recovered from historical source-lineage evidence as lineage. Facets cite verified source-import manifest rows; proof is hermetic/runtime lineage, not fresh USF proof.
aiGuidance: Source-backed draft. Facet descriptions are concise source-backed summaries citing verified manifest rows; proof is hermetic/stale, not current readiness. Enrich facet detail and publish fresh proof before generation; respect the hexagonal constraint in ADR 0005.

lifecycle.description: Manages provider configurations through an explicit lifecycle (proposed, configured, ready, degraded, disabled) so each provider instance is declared, validated, and promoted deliberately; it is provider-backed and database-backed but owns no tenant records. It deploys the V1-proven contract for GET/POST /api/admin/provider-configs plus the per-id lifecycle and delete operations and must keep its lifecycle/readiness model unchanged during extraction. Recovery re-runs proof:provider-config and proof:provider-readiness-contract before promotion continues.

stateModel.description: State includes providerKey, capability, environment, instanceLabel, classification, lifecycleState (proposed, configured, ready, degraded, disabled), endpoint, config, credentialRef, requiresCredential, and audit metadata. Database changes use ordered SQL migrations that must preserve this proven state model, and rollback is forward-fix or guarded restore after migration lineage is verified. Credentials are referenced rather than embedded, keeping secret material out of the configuration record.

permissions.description: Provider-config operations are administrative endpoints; the plane owns no tenant data, so standard platform security controls govern access rather than per-tenant roles. Credential references are gated by requiresCredential and resolved through the secrets boundary rather than exposed in config reads. Mutating the lifecycle of a provider is an operator action that must keep mutation boundaries closed on failure.

contracts.description: The interface contract is the provider-config repository behind a port (provider-config-repository) plus the admin create/read, lifecycle-transition, and delete operations. Configuration is read from the declared provider/config plane and missing or invalid configuration fails closed rather than inventing defaults. The lifecycleState transitions are the contractual states consumers depend on to know whether a provider is usable.

validation.description: Validation enforces required fields per provider, that credentialRef is present when requiresCredential is set, and that lifecycleState transitions are legal (for example, a provider cannot be ready without valid configuration). Inputs are validated before mutation and invalid configuration fails closed. Health is probed before a provider is allowed to reach the ready state.

errorModel.description: Provider failure returns a typed degraded/readiness failure, keeps mutation boundaries closed, and does not fabricate successful provider state; degraded mode disables side-effect paths that depend on the HTTP health-probe adapter while keeping read-only readiness and audit visible. The risk class is semantic drift or configuration loss rather than tenant data loss. A failed health probe moves the provider to degraded rather than reporting false readiness.

auditModel.description: State-changing provider-config operations follow audit-before-mutation behaviour with audit metadata on the record, capturing who changed a provider's configuration or lifecycle state. Audit and readiness signals remain visible in degraded mode. This preserves an honest, inspectable history of provider configuration changes.

readinessModel.description: Readiness is encoded directly in lifecycleState: a provider is ready only after it is configured, credentialed where required, and health-probed, and it degrades explicitly when the probe or substrate is unavailable. Readiness fails closed and stays visible read-only in degraded mode. Promotion is gated on proof:provider-config and proof:provider-readiness-contract passing.

proof.description: Behaviour is exercised by proof:provider-config and proof:provider-readiness-contract together with the cited auth-provider-config unit tests, demonstrating configuration, credential, lifecycle, and readiness semantics. Proof is hermetic/runtime lineage; fresh USF proof pending USF-101/USF-59.

uiSemanticDefinition.description: No verified UI source path in the manifest yet; author the UI semantic model before generation.
